rows.v: Spring Water, Artesian Water, Mineral Water, Well Water
TDS (Total Dissolved Solids) 0–50mg/L = very soft; 50–250 = light; 250–500 = medium; 500+ = hard mineral
Fine dining water service; pairing with food; revealing regional terroir of the source
Sparkling Mineral (natural CO₂), Sparkling Water (added CO₂), Seltzer (plain), Tonic Water (quinine-bitter)
Bubble size matters: fine persistent bubbles (Perrier) = cleaner; large loose bubbles (club soda) = less elegant
Palate cleansing between courses; mixing cocktails; digestif service
Purified/Filtered, Distilled, Reverse Osmosis, Alkaline, Electrolyte Water
Distilled ≈ 0mg/L (pure H₂O, flat taste); RO ≈ 1–10; Alkaline pH 8–9.5 (buffered)
Coffee and tea brewing (RO re-mineralized is ideal); laboratory use; electrolyte sports recovery
Hard (Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ rich): slightly chalky, mineral, can leave scale; Soft (Na⁺ treated): slippery mouthfeel, flat
Hard water → tea and beer brewing; Soft water → coffee (extracts more evenly); both affect soap lather

groups.name: Fine Dining Water Service

rows.v: Offer still and sparkling options; serve at 46–54°F; premium still in a decanter; pour and leave the bottle
Water TDS above 500mg/L can compete with delicate wines — match water minerality to food course weight

groups.name_es: Servicio de Agua en Alta Cocina

rows.v: Still: Evian (France, 357mg/L), Voss (Norway, 44mg/L), Acqua Panna (Italy, 260mg/L) | Sparkling: Perrier (France), San Pellegrino (Italy), Gerolsteiner (Germany, 2527mg/L)
Gerolsteiner is one of the world's most mineral-dense waters — excellent foil for rich fatty dishes
Delicate fish/sushi → low-TDS still (Voss); Rich steak/aged cheese → high-mineral still (Evian); Spicy food → lightly sparkling to cool the palate
The sommelier's rule: water should support food, not fight it — serve sparkling with fried or fatty; still with seafood
